draft.introduction: In Poetics 6, Aristotle defines tragedy and introduces the concept of catharsis: tragedy is an imitation of an action that, through pity and fear, effects the proper purging of these emotions. The meaning of catharsis remains among the most debated questions in literary theory.

translation.text: Tragedy, then, is an imitation of an action that is serious, complete, and of a certain magnitude; in language embellished with every kind of artistic ornament, the several kinds being found in separate parts of the play; in the form of action, not of narrative; through pity and fear effecting the proper catharsis of these emotions.
translation.original_text: ἔστιν οὖν τραγῳδία μίμησις πράξεως σπουδαίας καὶ τελείας

translator_notes.note: The term katharsis (κάθαρσις) has been interpreted as medical purgation, religious purification, and intellectual clarification. The Neoplatonists favored the purificatory reading, connecting tragic catharsis to the soul’s ethical and spiritual purification.
